text: This is a video presentation of endocyanin green fluorescence guided robotic-assisted transthoracic esophagectomy to evaluate the role of ICG-INR fluorescence imaging in real-time identification of thoracic duct during surgery and also to assess gastric cardiovascularity. The dose of ICG used is two point five milligram per mm. For thoracic duct identification site of injection is either first web space of bilateral lower limb or bilateral superficial inguinal lymph node. For gastric cardiovascularity intravenous injection is used. Bilateral superficial inguinal lymph nodes are identified using USG guidance and ICG is injected one ml each.

The patient is positioned prone for the infra-esophageal dissection, which begins anteriorly, allowing the esophagus to hang from the roof. The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament and continues dissection up to the diaphragm's crura and lower gastroesophageal junction. The subcarinal lymph node is carefully dissected to prevent thermal injury to the right and left main bronchus. Posterior dissection follows, with the thoracic duct identified using ICG Firefly technology. Fluorescence guidance aids in completing the infra-esophageal dissection.

The surgeon begins dissection in the supra-azygous region, carefully dissecting right paratracheal lymph nodes while preserving the right recurrent laryngeal nerve. Station six lymph nodes are cleared. On the left side, the left recurrent laryngeal nerve is identified and dissected, with the thoracic duct in close proximity. ICG fluorescence is used to identify and preserve the thoracic duct, preventing injury.

The surgeon dissects lymph nodes over the subclavian vessels, proceeding with slow and careful dissection in the supra-azygous region to avoid thermal injury to the membranous trachea. The azygos vein is clipped and divided, and the intact thoracic duct is confirmed. The mobilization of the intrathoracic esophagus is completed.

The patient is positioned supine for the interabdominal dissection. The surgeon incises the lesser omentum and continues dissection up to the diaphragm. The gastrosplenic ligament, containing short gastric vessels, is divided using a vessel sealer. The peritoneal capsule is opened, and station eight and twelve lymph nodes are dissected. The left gastric vessels are sealed and divided at their origin, completing the DII dissection with clearance of station XI P and XI D lymph nodes.

The surgeon carefully divides the greater omentum while preserving the gastroepiploic arcade. A gastric conduit is created using a stapler, and intravenous injection of dye confirms gastric vascularity. A side quadrangle esophagogastric anastomosis is performed in the neck using a powered stapler.

In our series of one sixty eight patients, anastomotic leak rates were two point nine percent and chylic rates were one point eight percent. Use of ICG fluorescence is easy and safe method for real time identification and preservation of thoracic duct. It also helps in assessing gastric conduit viability to avoid anastomotic leak. Thank you for watching this video.
